name: Giltedge
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding site near Maiden offering agate, chalcedony, and jasper collecting. Free access with 25 lb daily limit - perfect for Central Montana rock hunters.
description:
Giltedge is a accessible BLM rockhounding area in Fergus County near Maiden, Montana, located at moderate elevation in the transition zone between the Rocky Mountains and the Montana plains. This site provides excellent opportunity for collectors to find colorful agates, chalcedony specimens, and jasper fragments across open terrain. The area benefits from excellent exposed geology where Tertiary volcanic and sedimentary deposits have weathered to reveal quality specimens at the surface.

The Giltedge area sits within a region shaped by Miocene to Pliocene volcanic activity that created silica-rich deposits throughout Central Montana. Circulating groundwater deposited chalcedony and agate in fractures and cavities within volcanic rock, while weathering and erosion over millions of years has exposed these gemstones in accessible washes, hillsides, and rocky slopes. The moderate elevation and semi-arid climate have naturally concentrated specimens, making surface collecting productive.

Collectors at Giltedge can expect to find thumb-sized to fist-sized agate nodules, banded chalcedony pieces, and attractive jasper fragments scattered across the BLM land. The terrain is rolling with moderate slopes and scattered sagebrush typical of Central Montana. Most productive areas lie in drainage washes where water has naturally concentrated heavier stone over time. The site is beginner-friendly and suitable for family outings.

As public BLM land, Giltedge requires no permit and offers completely free access. The 25 lb per day personal use limit ensures sustainable collecting while allowing rockhounds to take home quality specimens. This is an ideal introduction to Montana rockhounding for those new to the hobby or visiting from out of state.
history: The Giltedge area lies within the northern Great Plains-Rocky Mountain transition zone, where Eocene to Miocene volcanic activity and silica-rich hydrothermal fluids created widespread agate and chalcedony deposits throughout Fergus County. These gemstones formed in volcanic rock and sedimentary sequences, with weathering and erosion over the past several million years exposing high-quality specimens that collectors find today in surface deposits and washes.
tips: Focus your search in dry washes and drainage channels where heavier agate and jasper naturally concentrate after spring snowmelt and rains. Look for translucent or banded specimens with warm earth tones - reds, oranges, creams, and browns indicate quality agates worth collecting. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and check multiple hillside exposures, as productive patches often appear in clusters rather than scattered randomly. The best specimens tend to hide under sagebrush, so systematically walk parallel lines across the terrain rather than randomly wandering.
terrain_notes: Elevation 5418 feet with moderate slopes across rolling terrain typical of Central Montana. Semi-arid grassland and sagebrush landscape with scattered rocky outcrops. Walking distance from parking typically 0.5 to 2 miles across open country with uneven footing and scattered loose rock. Well-draining soil means access is usually possible year-round, though spring and fall offer the most comfortable conditions.
best_season: April - October. Spring (April-May) and fall (September-October) offer ideal temperatures and lower fire danger. Summer months can bring occasional afternoon thunderstorms and heat, while winter snowfall may limit access depending on road conditions.
written_directions: From Maiden, take State Highway 191 northeast approximately 8 miles. Turn right onto a well-maintained gravel road and follow it north for approximately 3 miles until you reach BLM land marked with boundary signs. Look for a pullout area near sagebrush-covered hills on the left side. Park in the pullout - from here, the best collecting areas are within walking distance across open BLM land.
road_conditions: Gravel road in good condition, passable by standard passenger vehicles in dry conditions. Some washboard texture typical of rural Montana roads. No high-clearance required.
parking_notes: Pullout parking area near the property access point. Space for 3-5 vehicles. No formal facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. No digging that damages BLM land. Respect all boundary markers and adjacent private property.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
No destructive digging
Respect BLM boundaries and private property
nearest_services: Maiden (approximately 8 miles south) offers basic services. Lewiston (approximately 20 miles south) has gas, food, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain accessible to most hikers. Moderate slopes and uneven rocky ground require sturdy footwear. No wheelchair access. Sagebrush landscape is open and navigation is straightforward. Distances from parking to collecting areas are typically manageable for families with children age 6 and older.

faq.question: What kinds of rocks and gems can I find rockhounding at Giltedge near Maiden, Montana?
faq.answer: Giltedge is known for colorful agate nodules, chalcedony specimens, and jasper fragments. Most pieces range from thumb-sized to fist-sized and display warm earth tones including reds, oranges, creams, and browns. The best specimens are semi-translucent with banding patterns typical of quality Montana agates.

faq.question: Is there a fee or permit required for rockhounding at Giltedge, Montana?
faq.answer: No - Giltedge is on BLM public land and requires no permit for recreational rockhounding. There is zero collection fee. You can access the site for free as long as you follow BLM regulations including the 25 lb per day personal use limit.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound for agate at Giltedge?
faq.answer: April through October offers the most comfortable collecting conditions. Spring (April-May) and fall (September-October) are ideal with mild temperatures and lower fire danger. Summer can bring afternoon thunderstorms and heat, while winter snowfall may limit road access.

faq.question: How do I get to Giltedge rockhounding site from Maiden?
faq.answer: From Maiden, take State Highway 191 northeast for approximately 8 miles. Turn right onto a gravel road and follow it north for 3 miles until you see BLM boundary signs. Park in the pullout near sagebrush-covered hills. The best collecting areas are accessible within a short walk from parking.

faq.question: Is Giltedge, Montana a good rockhounding site for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes - Giltedge is ideal for beginners and families. The terrain is accessible with moderate slopes, no special skills required, and the 25 lb daily limit encourages responsible collecting. Children age 6 and older can comfortably participate. The open sagebrush landscape makes navigation straightforward.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need to rockhound at Giltedge effectively?
faq.answer: Bring a 5-gallon bucket for carrying specimens, sturdy hiking boots for uneven rocky terrain, a hand pick or small hammer for loosening specimens, a backpack with water, and sun protection. Search focus should be in dry washes and under sagebrush where agates naturally concentrate. Bring a GPS or mark your parking spot since terrain is open and navigation can be tricky returning to your vehicle.

meta_description: Free rockhounding at Giltedge BLM land near Maiden, Montana. Find agate, chalcedony, and jasper. No permit needed, 25 lb daily limit.
